stem: You are the GP in a regional clinic seeing a 14-year-old girl brought urgently by her mother. She is drowsy after 8 hours of fever, severe headache and one episode of vomiting. In the last 30 minutes a non-blanching petechial rash has appeared on her trunk and limbs. The nearest emergency department is 90 minutes away by road. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. She has no documented antibiotic allergy.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 °C, HR 130, RR 28, BP 92/56, SpO2 96% room air, GCS 14
data_table.Examination: Non-blanching petechial rash on trunk and limbs; warm peripheries; capillary refill 3 seconds; mild neck stiffness
data_table.Investigations: Bedside blood glucose 5.4 mmol/L; lactate not available; venous access not yet established

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate action in your clinic?

options.text: Administer intramuscular benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg now and arrange urgent retrieval transfer
options.rationale: Suspected meningococcaemia in a community setting warrants immediate intramuscular benzylpenicillin while awaiting transfer; intramuscular is acceptable when intravenous access is not yet available. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Wait for paramedic arrival to gain intravenous access before any antibiotic is given
options.rationale: Delaying antibiotics in suspected meningococcaemia worsens mortality; intramuscular benzylpenicillin should be given immediately when intravenous access is not yet established. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Give intravenous dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg and arrange retrieval transfer without antibiotic
options.rationale: Steroid alone has no role and antibiotic delay is dangerous; dexamethasone in meningitis is given with or shortly after the first antibiotic dose in hospital. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Prescribe oral ciprofloxacin chemoprophylaxis to the family and transfer the patient
options.rationale: Prophylaxis for contacts is given once the index case is stabilised; it does not treat the index patient and must not delay her own antibiotic. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Transfer the patient to hospital without any antibiotic to avoid blood culture contamination
options.rationale: Pre-hospital antibiotic improves survival in meningococcaemia and outweighs the modest reduction in culture yield; cultures can still be taken on arrival. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Suspected meningococcal disease in the community is a time-critical diagnosis. Australian guidelines indicate immediate intramuscular or intravenous benzylpenicillin if there is any delay establishing intravenous access. Survival correlates inversely with time to first antibiotic dose. After the first dose, urgent retrieval and supportive resuscitation with fluids and oxygen are arranged. Public health notification and ciprofloxacin prophylaxis for close contacts follow once the index case is stabilised.
key_points: Intramuscular benzylpenicillin in clinic if no intravenous access.
Do not delay first antibiotic dose for transfer or imaging.
Notify the public health unit on the same day.
Ciprofloxacin prophylaxis for household and kissing contacts.
explanation.pearls: Petechiae are a late sign; in early meningococcaemia the leg pain, cold mottled peripheries, and prolonged capillary refill precede the rash by hours and are the cues to act on suspicion alone.

subtopic_detail: Episodic viral wheeze in preschooler without maintenance ICS
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 2-year-old boy whose mother reports this is his fourth wheeze episode this winter, each triggered by a viral upper respiratory infection. Between episodes he is symptom-free, has no nocturnal cough and no exercise limitation. He has no eczema, no allergic rhinitis and no family history of atopy. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. He has had no oral steroid courses to date.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.8 °C, HR 130, RR 36, SpO2 96% room air
data_table.Examination: Mild expiratory wheeze bilaterally with minimal recession; alert and feeding well; no nasal flare; symmetric air entry
data_table.Investigations: Spirometry not feasible at this age; chest x-ray not indicated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Commence daily inhaled fluticasone 100 microgram twice daily via spacer for 12 weeks
options.rationale: Maintenance inhaled corticosteroids do not reduce viral-only wheeze episodes in preschoolers without atopic features and risk growth and adrenal effects. (Source: National Asthma Handbook)

options.text: Add daily oral montelukast and continue salbutamol as needed for symptom relief
options.rationale: Daily montelukast is not first-line for episodic viral wheeze and carries a black-box neuropsychiatric warning that limits use in young children. (Source: National Asthma Handbook)

options.text: Salbutamol via spacer as needed during episodes without maintenance inhaled corticosteroid
options.rationale: Episodic viral wheeze in a young preschooler without atopic features is managed with as-needed salbutamol via spacer; maintenance inhaled steroid is reserved for multi-trigger or atopic phenotypes. (Source: National Asthma Handbook)

options.text: Refer for allergy skin prick testing before any decision on inhaled treatment is made
options.rationale: Skin prick testing is reserved for suspected allergic asthma or multi-trigger phenotypes and is not needed for typical episodic viral wheeze. (Source: National Asthma Handbook)

options.text: Prescribe a 5-day course of oral prednisolone now and review again in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Oral steroids are reserved for moderate to severe acute wheeze; routine prophylactic courses are not recommended in mild episodic viral wheeze. (Source: National Asthma Handbook)

explanation.summary: Preschool wheeze is divided into episodic viral and multi-trigger phenotypes. Episodic viral wheeze without atopic background is managed with as-needed salbutamol via spacer during exacerbations and red flag advice. Maintenance inhaled corticosteroid is reserved for multi-trigger wheeze, persistent inter-episode symptoms or strong atopic background. Oral steroids in this age group are restricted to moderate or severe acute exacerbations.
key_points: Salbutamol via spacer is first-line during viral wheeze episodes.
Maintenance inhaled steroid only if multi-trigger or atopic.
Avoid oral steroid for mild episodic viral wheeze.
Review phenotype if episodes become more frequent.
explanation.pearls: A spacer with a face mask delivers more drug to the small airways than a nebuliser in a wriggly toddler; switching from nebuliser to spacer often improves practical response and reduces tachycardia from systemic absorption.

subtopic_detail: Septic arthritis of the hip discrimination from transient synovitis
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 5-year-old boy with 2 days of right hip pain, refusal to weight bear and progressive limp. He has had a low-grade temperature and looks unwell at triage. He has no recent trauma, no preceding viral illness and no rash. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.9 °C, HR 132, RR 24, BP 100/60, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Refusal to weight bear, hip held in flexion and external rotation, severe pain on log-roll and any internal rotation; no rash, no other joint involvement
data_table.Investigations: WCC 16.8 x10^9/L, CRP 95 mg/L, ESR 52 mm/h, blood culture pending; hip ultrasound shows joint effusion

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge with oral cefalexin and review in primary care in 48 hours if not better
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics and outpatient care will fail to clear an established septic joint and risk femoral head osteonecrosis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Repeat ultrasound in 24 hours and start treatment only if the effusion has grown
options.rationale: Serial imaging delays definitive treatment; the four Kocher criteria together give a very high probability of septic arthritis and warrant urgent joint aspiration. (Source: RCH CPG Septic Arthritis)

options.text: Trial of weight bearing with regular ibuprofen and outpatient orthopaedic review
options.rationale: Transient synovitis is a diagnosis of exclusion in a well child; the combination of fever, raised inflammatory markers and refusal to weight bear makes this unsafe. (Source: RCH CPG Septic Arthritis)

options.text: Start intravenous flucloxacillin without joint aspiration and reassess in 24 hours
options.rationale: Antibiotics before joint aspiration reduce culture yield and delay the source-control surgery that is required for septic arthritis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Urgent hip aspiration in theatre with washout and empirical intravenous flucloxacillin
options.rationale: Four positive Kocher criteria (fever above 38.5, refusal to bear weight, WCC above 12, ESR above 40) give over 95 percent probability of septic arthritis; aspiration, washout and intravenous antibiotics are the standard of care. (Source: RCH CPG Septic Arthritis)

explanation.summary: The Kocher criteria stratify the probability of septic arthritis of the hip in a child with limp: fever above 38.5, refusal to weight bear, WCC above 12 and ESR above 40. With all four present probability exceeds 95 percent. Australian practice favours urgent surgical aspiration and washout with empirical intravenous flucloxacillin pending cultures. Transient synovitis is a diagnosis of exclusion in a well, afebrile child with normal inflammatory markers and rapid improvement on rest.
key_points: Four Kocher criteria give over 95 percent probability of septic arthritis.
Aspiration in theatre obtains cultures and decompresses the joint.
Empirical flucloxacillin covers the commonest organisms.
Transient synovitis requires normal inflammatory markers.
explanation.pearls: Kingella kingae is now the commonest cause of culture-positive septic arthritis in children under 4 in Australia and is fastidious; if Gram stain is negative and the child is under 4, ask the laboratory to inoculate paediatric blood culture bottles with the joint aspirate to improve yield.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar in an antenatal clinic reviewing a 27-year-old RhD-negative G1P0 woman at 27+5 weeks with an uncomplicated singleton pregnancy. Booking antibody screen at 12 weeks and repeat at 27 weeks are both negative. She plans an unassisted vaginal birth at the same hospital. She has had no antenatal bleeding, no procedures and no abdominal trauma during the pregnancy.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/68, HR 76, SpO2 98%; symphysis-fundal height appropriate for gestation
data_table.Examination: Singleton pregnancy with cephalic presentation; fetal heart rate 142 by Doppler; abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Indirect Coombs negative at booking and at 27 weeks; group A RhD-negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate anti-D plan?

options.text: Defer anti-D until the postnatal cord blood result confirms a RhD-positive infant
options.rationale: Postnatal anti-D alone is insufficient; routine antenatal anti-D at 28 and 34 weeks prevents the residual third-trimester sensitisation risk. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Give 625 IU intramuscular anti-D now at 28 weeks and again at 34 weeks regardless of events
options.rationale: Australian National Blood Authority guidance recommends routine antenatal anti-D 625 IU at 28 and 34 weeks for all RhD-negative women without preformed antibodies, plus postnatal dosing if the cord blood is RhD-positive. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Give a single 1500 IU intramuscular anti-D dose at 28 weeks with no further antenatal dose
options.rationale: Single high-dose schedules are used overseas but Australian practice is two 625 IU doses at 28 and 34 weeks; a single dose risks waning cover before delivery. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Withhold antenatal anti-D and give only after sensitising events such as bleeding or trauma
options.rationale: Event-driven dosing alone leaves silent fetomaternal haemorrhage uncovered; routine antenatal anti-D is added to event-driven doses, not substituted by them. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Wait until 34 weeks and give a single antenatal anti-D dose then with postnatal follow-up
options.rationale: A 34-week single dose does not cover the third trimester from 28 weeks onwards; both doses are required to prevent sensitisation. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Routine antenatal anti-D prophylaxis prevents RhD sensitisation that would otherwise threaten future pregnancies through haemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn. The Australian National Blood Authority recommends 625 IU intramuscular anti-D at 28 and 34 weeks for all RhD-negative women without preformed antibodies. Additional anti-D is given after sensitising events such as antepartum haemorrhage, external cephalic version, abdominal trauma or invasive procedures, with a Kleihauer-Betke test to titrate dosing if the bleed is large. A further dose is given postnatally within 72 hours if the baby is RhD-positive.
key_points: Routine doses are 625 IU at 28 and 34 weeks.
Sensitising events require additional anti-D within 72 hours.
Postnatal dose follows a RhD-positive cord blood result.
Kleihauer titrates dosing when bleeds may exceed standard cover.
explanation.pearls: NIPT for fetal RHD genotype is now available privately in Australia and can spare anti-D to women carrying RhD-negative babies; in the public system universal anti-D remains the default until fetal RHD genotyping is publicly funded.

subtopic_detail: Vaginal birth after caesarean contraindicated by classical scar
stem: You are the obstetric registrar counselling a G2P1 woman at 26+0 weeks. Her first pregnancy ended in a classical (vertical upper-segment) caesarean section at 30+0 weeks for a footling breech presentation with extreme prematurity. She has no other comorbidities, a singleton cephalic pregnancy on today's ultrasound and a posterior placenta clear of the os. She has been reading about vaginal birth after caesarean and asks about her options.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72, HR 78, SpO2 98%; afebrile; symphysis-fundal height appropriate
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen with low transverse Pfannenstiel skin scar; uterus soft; cervix long and closed on speculum
data_table.Investigations: Ultrasound: singleton cephalic at 26 weeks, posterior placenta clear of os, normal liquor volume

lead_in: What mode of birth should be recommended?

options.text: Plan a trial of vaginal birth after caesarean from 39 weeks with continuous CTG
options.rationale: Trial of vaginal birth carries a 4 to 9 percent uterine rupture risk after a classical scar, far above the 0.5 percent risk after a low transverse scar. (Source: RANZCOG VBAC Statement)

options.text: Offer vaginal birth after caesarean only if her cervix is favourable at 39 weeks
options.rationale: Cervical favourability does not alter the rupture risk of a classical scar; trial of labour remains contraindicated regardless of Bishop score. (Source: RANZCOG VBAC Statement)

options.text: Plan induction of labour at 39 weeks with prostaglandins and oxytocin augmentation
options.rationale: Prostaglandins and oxytocin in a woman with a classical scar markedly increase rupture risk and are contraindicated. (Source: RANZCOG VBAC Statement)

options.text: Plan elective caesarean section between 36+0 and 37+0 weeks with antenatal corticosteroids
options.rationale: Classical caesarean scar carries a high rupture risk that rises with established labour; elective birth between 36+0 and 37+0 weeks balances rupture risk and neonatal maturity. (Source: RANZCOG VBAC Statement)

options.text: Plan external cephalic version at 37 weeks followed by elective caesarean only if breech
options.rationale: Mode of birth is determined by uterine scar rather than fetal presentation; she should not labour at all after a classical scar. (Source: RANZCOG VBAC Statement)

explanation.summary: A classical (vertical) upper-segment uterine incision contraindicates vaginal birth in subsequent pregnancies because labour rupture risk is 4 to 9 percent compared with 0.5 percent after low transverse scars. RANZCOG recommends elective caesarean between 36+0 and 37+0 weeks with antenatal corticosteroids to balance fetal maturity and rupture risk. Prostaglandins and oxytocin further raise rupture risk and must be avoided. The previous indication (extreme prematurity necessitating classical incision) does not change the scar geometry for future risk.
key_points: Classical scar is an absolute contraindication to vaginal birth.
Elective caesarean at 36+0-37+0 weeks balances risks.
Antenatal corticosteroids cover lung maturation at this gestation.
Prostaglandin or oxytocin use is contraindicated.
explanation.pearls: Take 30 seconds at every preconception or early antenatal visit to confirm the upper segment scar status from operative notes; many women record only 'previous caesarean' on paperwork and a classical scar can be missed until labour.
